title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Lord Kettlem and Bondel discuss the death of Gerry Wade, which occurred in Bondel's room. While the police, specifically Detective Largen, suspect a political motive or non-normal cause, the death is officially considered an accident or suicide. Tredwell reported that investigators found only Wade's fingerprints. Lord Kettlem mentions that Wade has a much younger half-sister.

Bondel attempts to assert authority over the gardener, McDonald, by demanding he clear the overgrown tennis court grass and pick grapes out of season, though he responds ambiguously. Later, Lord Kettlem reveals details regarding an "alarm clock incident": vacationers had reportedly hidden numerous alarm clocks in Wade's room as a joke. Following Wade's death, the clocks were found arranged on a mantelpiece, though the servants denied touching them.

That night, Bondel reflects on the strangeness of the clocks. She recalls a detail from her maid: seven clocks were arranged on the mantelpiece, while an eighth clock was found in the grass outside the window. Contemplating whether Wade threw the clock before dying, she concludes it was unlikely given his state of unconsciousness. Seeking clarity, she decides to contact Bill Everleigh.

While attempting to write to Bill, Bondel finds a wrinkled, unfinished letter stuck in her desk drawer. The letter is dated September 21st, the night before Wade was found dead. Addressed to Lorena, the note mentions that Wade is feeling well but tells her to forget about "the seven clock thing," which he previously thought was a joke. The letter ends abruptly after Wade begins to mention someone named Rachel. Bondel notes two specific phrases in the text: "I'm feeling well" and "I'm so tired, my eyes won't stay open."
